input.headline: The Thing With Feathers review – well-intentioned adaptation of Max Porter novella about grief

raw.headline: The Thing With Feathers review – well-intentioned adaptation of Max Porter novella about grief

winner: I watched a well-intentioned adaptation of a novella about grief and felt so moved by the emotional weight of it that I’m currently grieving the fact that I have to go to work tomorrow.

candidates.text: I tried to read the review of The Thing With Feathers, but I had to put it down because every time I turned a page, a pigeon tried to nest in my hair.
candidates.judge_raw: {"score": 8.5, "reason": "Creative and specific joke that ties the headline to a humorous image, with a surprising twist involving pigeons and hair."}

candidates.text: I watched *The Thing with Feathers* and now I'm convinced that grief isn't a metaphor—it's just a bird that refuses to fly away no matter how many times you yell "Go on!" at it.
candidates.judge_raw: {"score": 8.5, "reason": "Creative metaphor, concise, surprising, and ties directly to the headline's theme of grief."}

candidates.text: This movie wasn't just a feather duster, it was a feather *broom* of existential dread.
candidates.judge_raw: {"score": 8.5, "reason": "Playful wordplay with 'feather duster' and 'feather broom,' ties into the headline's mention of 'the thing with feathers,' and effectively conveys the heavy tone of the movie."}

candidates.text: I tried to read "The Thing With Feathers" while wearing a bird costume, but grief just kept flying out of the pages and landing on my head.
candidates.judge_raw: {"score": 8.2, "reason": "Surprising and specific metaphor with a clever twist on the theme of grief."}

candidates.text: The thing with feathers finally got a movie — and now everyone's debating whether it's a metaphor for grief or just a really confused bird with poor life choices.
candidates.judge_raw: {"score": 8.5, "reason": "Puns on 'the thing with feathers' (a metaphor for grief in the book) and adds a humorous twist by suggesting it's a confused bird, showing both specificity and surprise."}

candidates.text: I watched a well-intentioned adaptation of a novella about grief and felt so moved by the emotional weight of it that I’m currently grieving the fact that I have to go to work tomorrow.
candidates.judge_raw: {"score": 8.5, "reason": "Surprising twist on the premise, specific to the context of the review, and effectively uses irony for humor."}

candidates.text: Why did the feathered critic write such a mixed review? Because he was too *plucked* to give it a clean bill of health!
candidates.judge_raw: {"score": 7.5, "reason": "The joke is clever with the pun on 'plucked' and ties to the headline's mention of feathers. It’s specific and surprising, though the connection to grief in the novella isn't directly addressed."}

candidates.text: The thing with feathers is a terrible metaphor for grief—it's just a bird that keeps showing up at your door and asking if you've seen its nest.
candidates.judge_raw: {"score": 7.5, "reason": "The joke is concise, specific, and uses a surprising twist on the metaphor of 'the thing with feathers.' It ties directly to the headline's theme of grief while adding a quirky, relatable image. However, it could be more original or punchier."}

candidates.text: I heard the adaptation of a novella about grief was a bit of a downer, but at least it has some feather-weight appeal.
candidates.judge_raw: {"score": 7.5, "reason": "Puns on 'feather-weight' and the context of grief provide a decent surprise and specificity, though the joke is slightly generic and could be tighter."}
